body: {"answer": "Glaucoma is a group of eye diseases that damage the optic nerve and can lead to vision loss and blindness. The damage usually results from a gradual rise in the fluid pressure inside the eye when the clear fluid (aqueous humor) does not drain properly. The most common form is open\u2011angle glaucoma, in which the fluid drains too slowly through the mesh\u2011like drainage system at the angle where the cornea and iris meet, causing pressure to build up and injure the optic nerve. Glaucoma often has no early symptoms; it typically begins with loss of peripheral (side) vision, creating a \u201ctunnel\u2011vision\u201d effect, and can eventually affect straight\u2011ahead vision if untreated. It is a leading cause of blindness in the United States, especially among people over 60, African Americans over 40, Mexican Americans over 60, and those with a family history of the disease. While there is no cure, early diagnosis and treatment\u2014usually with prescription eye drops and/or surgery\u2014can control the pressure and help preserve remaining vision."}
